raw_text.question:
Imagine you have a family tree that shows the lineage from ancestors to their descendants, with arrows pointing from parents to children. Can this family tree, with its directed lineage paths, be accurately represented without the arrows while still maintaining the correct relationships? True or false?

Retrieved documents:
[Document 1]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Girth]
Let $G$ be a graph.
The '''girth''' of $G$ is the smallest length of any cycle in $G$.
An acyclic graph is defined as having a girth of infinity.
Category:Definitions/Graph Theory
\end{definition}

[Document 2]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Vector Subtraction/Arrow Representation]
Let $\mathbf u$ and $\mathbf v$ be vector quantities of the same physical property.
Let $\mathbf u$ and $\mathbf v$ be represented by arrows embedded in the plane such that:
:$\mathbf u$ is represented by $\vec {AB}$
:$\mathbf v$ is represented by $\vec {AC}$
that is, so that the initial point of $\mathbf v$ is identified with the initial point of $\mathbf u$.
:300px
Then their '''(vector) difference''' $\mathbf u - \mathbf v$ is represented by the arrow $\vec {CB}$.
\end{definition}

[Document 3]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Logical Argument]
A '''logical argument''' (or just '''argument''') is a process of creating a new statement from one or more existing statements.
An '''argument''' proceeds from a set of premises to a conclusion, by means of logical implication, via a procedure called logical inference.
An '''argument''' may have more than one premise, but only one conclusion.
While statements may be classified as either '''true''' or '''false''', an '''argument''' may be classified as either valid or invalid.
Loosely speaking, a valid argument is one that leads unshakeably from true statements to other true statements, whereas an invalid argument is one that can lead you to, for example, a false statement from one which is true.
Thus:
: An argument may be valid, even though its premises are false.
: An argument may be invalid, even though its premises are true.
: An argument may be invalid ''and'' its premises false.
To be sure of the truth of a conclusion, it is necessary to make sure both that the premises are true ''and'' that the '''argument''' is valid.
However, while you may not actually know whether a statement is true or not, you can investigate the consequences of it being either true or false, and see what effect that has on the truth value of the proposition(s) of which it is a part. That, in short, is what the process of '''logical argument''' consists of.
An '''argument''' may be described symbolically by means of sequents, which specify the flow of an '''argument'''.
\end{definition}

[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Acyclic Graph]
An '''acyclic graph''' is a graph or digraph with no cycles.
An acyclic connected undirected graph is a tree.
An acyclic disconnected undirected graph is a forest.
Category:Definitions/Graph Theory
\end{definition}

[Document 5]
\section{König's Tree Lemma}
Tags: König's Tree Lemma, Tree Theory, Graph Theory, Trees

\begin{theorem}
Let $T$ be a rooted tree with an infinite number of nodes, each with a finite number of children.
Then $T$ has a branch of infinite length.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We will show that we can choose an infinite sequence of nodes $t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots$ of $T$ such that:
* $t_0$ is the root node;
* $t_{n+1}$ is a child of $t_n$;
* Each $t_n$ has infinitely many descendants.
Then the sequence $t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots$ is such a branch of infinite length.
Take the root node $t_0$.
By definition, it has a finite number of children.
Suppose that all of these childen had a finite number of descendants.
Then that would mean that $t_0$ had a finite number of descendants, and that would mean $T$ was finite.
So $t_0$ has at least one child with infinitely many descendants.
Thus, we may pick $t_1$ as any one of those children.
Now, suppose node $t_k$ has infinitely many descendants. 
As $t_k$ has a finite number of children, by the same argument as above, $t_k$ has at least one child with infinitely many descendants.
Thus we may pick $t_{k+1}$ which has infinitely many descendants.
The assertion follows by the Axiom of Dependent Choice.
{{improve|It doesn't take much to modify this proof to use the axiom of countable choice for finite sets.}}
{{qed}}
\end{proof}
raw_text.response:
False.

A family tree, when represented as a directed graph where the direction of the edges indicates the parent-child relationship, cannot be accurately represented without the arrows while still maintaining the correct relationships. The direction of the edges is crucial because it specifies the generational order and the nature of the relationships between individuals. Without the arrows, the graph becomes undirected, and the specific lineage and generational hierarchy that defines a family tree would be lost. This transformation could result in ambiguity regarding who is a parent and who is a child, thus failing to maintain the correct relationships.
